dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at 1321 Happy Street, Richmond.
Hello, I'm Jay, an EMT. What are you experiencing today?
I'm struggling to breathe and the shortness of breath started about 30 minutes ago.
Mr. Roberts, can you tell me your name and where we are?
Your eyes are open and tracking well.
How are you feeling right now?
Can you squeeze my fingers to show me you can move your hands?
Can you speak or cough? This helps us know your airway is clear.
Yes, I can - it's tight, but I'm breathing.
Your breathing is very fast and sounding wheezy on both sides.
Mr. Roberts, let's check your pulse. Can you keep still while I do this?
You mentioned shortness of breath. When exactly did it start?
I have trouble breathing most mornings, but this is the worst it's felt for about 30 minutes now.
Have you had increased swelling in your legs or trouble breathing lying flat?
Yes, I have had a lot of swelling in my legs and I sleep on two pillows because I can't lie flat.
Have you been taking your heart or blood pressure medications?
Yes, I have high blood pressure and take medication for it every day.
Are you experiencing any pain in addition to the breathing problems?
No pain, just very short on breath.
Your blood pressure is 165 over 84.
Your oxygen saturation is 83%.
Your heart rate is 127 beats per minute, which is quite elevated and consistent with sinus tachycardia.
Your breathing rate is 36 per minute, which is very high.
Mr. Roberts, we're placing you on a four-lead EKG to monitor your heart.
We applied a Nitroglycerin patch, but your symptoms haven't improved yet.
We're giving you a nebulizer treatment with Albuterol and Atrovent to help open your airways.
You use home oxygen and have wheezing. You also have a history of COPD, CHF, sleep apnea, GERD, and diabetes. How are those conditions doing now?
The EKG shows a rapid heart rate with some changes in the heart's electrical activity.
The nebulizer has been started, and you should begin to feel some improvement.
Your oxygen level has improved to 99%, and your breathing is now 28 per minute - things are starting to get better.
We're going to insert an IV and administer furosemide to help reduce fluid in your lungs.
Your breathing is getting better with the nebulizer and we're applying CPAP now.
Your blood pressure is now 187 over 95 and your oxygen saturation remains at 99%.
I'm now doing a detailed 12-lead EKG to get a better look at your heart's condition.
Your blood sugar is 165, which is a normal reading for now.
I've contacted Medical Control to inform them about your condition and the steps we've taken.
We need to transport you to VAMC Richmond for further care. Would you be okay with that, Mr. Roberts?
We will now safely secure you on the stretcher and get you to the hospital.
Mr. Roberts, your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15 - meaning you're conscious and able to respond accurately.
Have you been taking your medications for COPD or diabetes lately?
I haven't taken my fluid pills in three days.
What medications do you take regularly and have you had any allergic reactions?
I use Prednisone, amlodipine, Symbicort, Lantus, Omeprazole, cetirizine, montelukast, and Tylenol. I have no known allergies.
We'll check your skin for any rashes, burns, or bite marks. Do you see anything unusual?
No, my skin looks normal, no changes or bites that I've noticed.
Your pupils appear normal, and you don't have any signs of neck discomfort in our assessment.
I'll listen to your lungs now. You're wheezing but have some reduced breath sounds.
We'll check your arms and legs for strength and any visible injuries.
Your peripheral pulses are present, and there is no noticeable swelling or injury on your limbs.
The nebulizer has delivered a combination of Albuterol and Atrovent to open your airways.
I'll give you a few more minutes to let the medication work effectively.
Your heart rate is now 124, and your oxygen levels are still holding at 99%.
Your breathing is 28 per minute - much better than earlier.
Your SpO2 is still 99% with CPAP - things are continuing to improve.
The EKG continues to show a rapid heart rate with no new changes, still consistent with sinus tachycardia.
We are ready to transport you with CPAP in place and continuous EKG monitoring.
Your BP is now 177/90 and your heart rate is stable at 121.
The furosemide has been administered, and we're continuing to monitor for fluid improvement.
Your glucose is undetectable, which is not uncommon for someone on insulin.
Your GCS remains 15 - you remain conscious and aware of your surroundings.
You are secured on the stretcher. I'll confirm hospital readiness now.
VAMC Richmond has been alerted and is prepared to receive you.
You'll be transferred on the stretcher with a sheet once we arrive at the hospital.
We've prepared everything for transport, and vitals continue to be monitored.
SpO2 is 98%, and your breathing rate remains at 33 - manageable with CPAP.
The CPAP continues to support stable breathing.
Mr. Roberts, can you tell me what day it is and the city we're in?
Thursday, and we're in Richmond.
Your awareness and coordination remain consistent.
Hospital protocols are in place, and they'll be waiting at the stretcher.
VAMC Richmond is the best destination for further medical management.
You'll be transferred to a stretcher with sheet once we arrive at the hospital.
We're continuing to follow Medical Control directions for patient care.
Your oxygen level is still 99%, and all signs suggest you're stable.
All medications and monitoring steps have been documented and confirmed.
You are secured and ready for safe transport.
Your GCS remains 15 - awareness and alertness unchanged.
Continue using CPAP. I'll ensure your airway remains secure and breathing monitored.
Your pulse is still 121, and the rhythm remains regular with sinus tachycardia.
Your oxygen saturation is still 99% - excellent for now.
The EKG continues to show a rapid heart rate with no new findings.
We'll continue to monitor your blood glucose during the ride.
We're transmitting your vitals and procedures in real-time to the ER.
Your care has included Nitroglycerin, nebulizers, furosemide, and continuous monitoring.
Our actions are consistent with EMS protocols for pulmonary edema and CHF.
Our treatment aligns with standard care for patients in respiratory distress.
We're en route with a roughly 10-minute ETA to VAMC Richmond.
The hospital has confirmed they will meet you at the stretcher.
The EKG, monitoring, and CPAP setup are all in place for transport.
Transfer of care is scheduled and hospital staff are prepared.
You'll be transferred to the stretcher via sheet - can you stay still for that?
All set for transfer and movement is clear.
Final vitals are SpO2 at 98, BP 177/90, HR 121, Respirations 33 - no change noted.
No issues during transfer. Ready for delivery to hospital.
The hospital nurse will receive you directly from here.
Patient is stable and under observation for transport.
Updating hospital regarding your condition and improvement steps now.
All monitoring and care tools are secure and functioning.
Patient was transferred without any issues.
Final vitals and care notes transmitted to ER upon arrival.
I have transmitted the written summary to the hospital on fax as well.
Handoff finalized at 09:40. Patient is en route.
Mental capacity remains stable: alert, aware of person, place, and time.
Yes, I understand everything still.
